quiz.title: Macroeconomics (Quiz 4)
quiz.subtitle: Unit 4 Quiz 4: Business Cycle Theories, Inflation, and Deflation (13 Questions)
quiz.subject: Economics
quiz.topic: New Classical, New Keynesian, Real Business Cycle Theory, Demand-Pull Inflation, Cost-Push Inflation, Stagflation, Deflation

questions.question: Which business cycle theory states that only unexpected fluctuations in aggregate demand bring fluctuations in real GDP around potential GDP?
questions.explanation: New classical theory emphasizes rational expectations. Only unanticipated changes in aggregate demand can cause real GDP to deviate from potential GDP because anticipated changes are already factored into wages and prices.

questions.question: Which type of fluctuations in aggregate demand do the theories say create business cycles? The fluctuations in the ________ cycle theory are unexpected while the fluctuations in the ________ cycle theory are both unexpected and expected.
questions.explanation: New classical theory holds that only unexpected AD fluctuations affect real GDP (due to rational expectations). New Keynesian theory, with its emphasis on sticky wages and prices, allows both expected and unexpected AD changes to have real effects.

questions.question: In which business cycle theory does a decrease in productivity growth play a major role? According to ________ theory, a decrease in productivity growth shifts the ________.
options: real business cycle; AD curve rightward
real business cycle; demand for loanable funds curve leftward
real business cycle; AD curve leftward
questions.explanation: Real Business Cycle (RBC) theory attributes cycles to real shocks like productivity changes. A decrease in productivity growth reduces the expected return on investment, shifting the demand for loanable funds curve leftward and lowering the real interest rate.

questions.question: Which of the following decisions and on what they depend creates the key ripple effect in real business cycle theory? The ________ decision and it depends on the ________.
options: when-to-work; rigidity of the money wage rate
questions.explanation: In RBC theory, the key mechanism is intertemporal substitution of labour. Workers decide when to work based on the real interest rate. A higher real interest rate makes working now (and saving the earnings) more attractive than working later.

questions.question: According to real business cycle theory, workers' decisions to work now versus later depend on which of the following variables?
options: the real wage rate today but not the real wage rate in the future
questions.explanation: RBC theory emphasizes the real interest rate as the key determinant of labour supply over time. A higher real interest rate increases the return to working now and saving, so labour supply increases today.

questions.question: "Intertemporal substitution" in real business cycle theory refers to the change in which of the following variables as a result of the change in the real interest rate?
questions.explanation: Intertemporal substitution refers to the shifting of labour supply between time periods in response to changes in the real interest rate. A higher real interest rate incentivizes working more today (and enjoying leisure later), increasing the current supply of labour.

questions.question: The economy starts out at a full-employment equilibrium. Some events then occur that generate a demand-pull inflation. An increase in all of the following events except which one might start a demand-pull inflation?
questions.explanation: Demand-pull inflation is caused by increases in aggregate demand (AD). Increases in exports, money supply, government spending, and transfer payments all boost AD. An increase in the money wage rate raises production costs and shifts SRAS leftward, causing cost-push inflation, not demand-pull.

questions.question:
Suppose that the money prices of raw materials rise. With no action by government or the Bank of Canada, which of the following outcomes will occur?

(I) Aggregate demand increases and the price level rises.
(II) Aggregate demand increases and aggregate supply decreases.
(III) Initially, employment decreases and the price level rises.
questions.explanation: A rise in raw material prices is a negative supply shock. It shifts the SRAS curve leftward. This raises the price level and reduces real GDP, which means employment initially decreases. Aggregate demand does not increase; it remains unchanged. Therefore, only statement III is correct.

questions.question: How does demand-pull inflation begin?
options: The price of oil rises sharply.
The central bank cuts the interest rate.
Investment decreases as expected future profits fall.
Government expenditure on goods and services decrease.
questions.explanation: Demand-pull inflation is initiated by an increase in aggregate demand. A central bank cutting interest rates stimulates consumption and investment spending, shifting the AD curve rightward and pulling up the price level.

questions.question: Which of the following events can kick start cost-push inflation?
options: an increase in the money wage rate
questions.explanation: Cost-push inflation results from a decrease in aggregate supply (leftward shift of SRAS). An increase in the money wage rate raises firms' production costs, shifting SRAS leftward and pushing the price level up.

questions.question: Which of the following events will start a stagflation?
options: a leftward shift of the demand curve
a rightward shift of the demand curve
a leftward shift of the short-run aggregate supply curve
a rightward shift of the short-run aggregate supply curve
a rightward shift of the long-run aggregate supply curve
questions.explanation: Stagflation is the combination of stagnation (falling real GDP, rising unemployment) and inflation (rising price level). A leftward shift of the SRAS curve (a negative supply shock) causes exactly this: lower output and higher prices.

questions.question: Which of the following outcomes is a consequence of an unanticipated deflation?
options: an increasing growth rate of potential GDP
questions.explanation: Unanticipated deflation means the price level falls more than expected. If nominal wages are sticky, the real wage rate ($W/P$) rises because the denominator ($P$) falls. This increases real labour costs to firms, potentially reducing employment.

questions.question: Which of the following actions will end deflation?
options: an increase in the growth rate of the money stock
a one-time increase in the quantity of money
a rise in the interest rate
a decrease in the growth rate of the money stock
a one-time decrease in the quantity of money
questions.explanation: Deflation is a sustained decrease in the price level, often caused by slow money growth. To end deflation, the central bank must persistently increase the growth rate of the money stock to boost aggregate demand and reverse falling price expectations.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered the major business cycle theories and the distinctions between demand-pull, cost-push, and deflationary dynamics.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! You understand the core differences between new classical, new Keynesian, and real business cycle theories. Review the mechanisms of intertemporal substitution.
scoreMessages.50: A fair attempt. Focus on: 1) New Classical = only unexpected AD matters. 2) New Keynesian = both expected and unexpected AD matter. 3) RBC = real shocks and intertemporal substitution. 4) Demand-pull = AD shifts right. 5) Cost-push = SRAS shifts left.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Remember: Stagflation requires a leftward SRAS shift. Deflation raises the real wage if nominal wages are sticky. To end deflation, increase the growth rate of the money stock persistently.
